title: Unapologetically Intro
lyrics:
A lot has happened, shifted, grown and changed since The First Time was released and I introduced myself to you. I've gotten the thrill of hearing my songs on the radio, played stages that my idols left their legacy on, travelled the world, fought off some youthful insecurities , shrugged through some new ones, felt my heart ache, felt it beat again, lost what I thought was love, until I found what love really is. It's been the most thrilling and difficult chapter of my life. What I can tell you is that I wrote my heart out. It's all here, just as it happened. I felt that if I let myself be vulnerable and sometime bitterly honest, maybe you would hear yourself in this record.
So, Unapologetically, here it is...
Kelsea

title: Kelsea Ballerini defines “Sorry Mom”

"Sorry Mom" is also one of my favorite songs on the record, um
It's about just this beautiful change that’s happened in my life the last couple of years where
My relationship with my mom has kind of pivoted from mother-daughter to woman-to-woman
And, um, that's allowed a lot of these really open beautiful conversations between us

I think every parent just wants to, like, bubble wrap their kids, you know?

title: Kelsea Ballerini defines “WAIT!”
lyrics:
You know, it's funny though, this album, I do wanna say
I never write anything for shock and awe
That's never my purpose as a, a songwriter
Everything that feels like a little edgy or using a curse word or whatever
It is, it is for the point of the song to, to get across
And I think a lot of artists will put certain things in songs
Just for the shock and awe and that, that is not what I did, um
It's because it's, that, that was the correct lyric to get the emotion across
But, um, "WAIT!" is, it's kind of me
Looking in the mirror and acknowledging
That I have this massive fear of, um, being left, so I leave
And that could be in any form in my life
Um, I'm very scared of something ending
Whether it's like a career thing or a friendship or a relationship
So if I see it start to go a little south, even if it's salvageable
I'll just jump ship to just save myself
Even though at the end of the day, I'm not saving myself
I'm robbing myself of something that could be beautiful, so
"WAIT!" is that moment of acknowledging that and being like
"Okay, I know I told you to leave, but can you turn around and come back now?
My bad"

title: Kelsea Ballerini defines “Two Things”
lyrics:
"Two things can be true" is actually a phrase that my therapist told me years ago, uhm, that
Sometimes, actually, often times, there is space for more than one thing to have value, and, uhm
It's helped me a lot
But the song itself is really about kind of this moment in your relationship where you're really, you're kind of, you're in it
And your, your old patterns are coming up and showing up
And you have to make that decision to either, you know, learn to go from fighting with to fighting for, or not
And it's kind of that, like, intense crossroads moment, and I'm proud of this song
Even from a production standpoint, because we really tried to capture that intensity
Uhm, and, yeah, I think it's, it's a big kind of growing pain lessened, and I'm so proud of it

title: Kelsea Ballerini defines “How Much Do You Love Me”
lyrics:
"How Much Do You Love Me" is one of the last songs that I wrote for this record, um
It's kind of a weirdly very vulnerable song because it's, it's saying
How much you love this person and just hoping that they feel the same way
and, and it's kind of in the dramatics of it
it's like if the world was ending and the meteor hit
like, would you drive to me to die with me?
Like if I, if I went insane and I didn't remember my name
would you still remind me? Would you stay beside me?
And so it's like not in the small moments
It's like these big grandiose thoughts of like
"I love you that much, do you love me that much?"
Um, and it's kind of a vulnerable thing to ask somebody, but, um
I think there's like a tenderness and an honesty in that that I'm really proud of

title: Kelsea Ballerini defines “Deep”
lyrics:
This is the song that I was the most nervous to play my mother, for sure
But, you know what? Here's the thing
This, this album is made by a 30-year-old woman, 31 now
And I made it with four other women, and so
There is a theme of womanhood and girlhood throughout the whole thing, and
One thing about growing up is, just being confident in what makes you feel good
And being confident in your own skin, and
I think for me, I always kind of shied away from writing about certain subjects
And for, for me, "Deep" is that song where it's like, just kind of committing to the idea that girls can sing about the things that guys sing about too

title: Kelsea Ballerini defines “Did You Make It Home? (outro)”
lyrics:
"Did You Make It Home?"
It's now an outro, it's like one minute
Uh, but actually, it was the original title of the album until Patterns was born
And then, we quickly were like, "Oh yeah, Patterns really fits the whole kind of thesis of the record"
But I wanted to leave this on as an outro just because it feels like
The sentiment, like, the sentiment that I want people to leave the record with
And feeling, which is like whatever you got from this fourteen-song journey
Did you get to where you're going? Are you safe?
I think that's the true testament of love, it's when you text someone and you call someone and ask if they made it home safe, so
That was the message that I wanted to leave everyone with

title: Kelsea Ballerini defines “First Rodeo”
lyrics:
"First Rodeo" is kind of the first love song that I wrote for this album
I don't tradtitionally write a lot of love songs
I, I really love, I love writing sad songs and heartbreak
I think it's easier to tap into those emotions, but
"Rodeo" I think was really special to me because
It was acknowledging the last chapter of my life, it was like
Listen, I love love so much, like, I am a relationship girlie
and, um, this song just says, you know
"I, I was on the horse and I rode off into the sunset
and it didn't work out and then I fell off the horse and so, like
yeah, I'm a little dusty and, yeah, like my jeans have some rips in 'em now
but, like, I love love and I'd love to get back on the horse
if, if, like, if you'll just take it slow with me, like, be patient with me
I have to, like, I have to shake this off still"
like, "Will you help me?" basically, um
and it, it's just such an honest song for where I was at at that point
of, of really hopeful and really eager to just fully fall back in love
but being timid as you are after, after, you know, heartbreak

title: Kelsea Ballerini defines “Patterns”
lyrics:
So let's kick things off with the title track
The song "Patterns" feels like the perfect kickoff to this record
because thematically, it really is like a self-assessment
I think something happens as you grow up
especially as you get into your thirties where
you look at yourself differently, you assess your life differently
and you find out what's important to you
what you wanna move forward into the next chapter of your life with and
I mean, that's what the whole album is
but specificially this song, "Patterns," it's, it's
that kind of blissful acceptance of what makes you who you are
and the good and the bad and the ugly and the beautiful of all of that
and saying like, "Alright, there's some things about me that maybe
I could work on, but there's also things about me that I love"
and that's the human experience and celebrating all of it

title: Kelsea Ballerini defines “Cowboys Cry Too”
lyrics:
"Cowboys Cry Too"
I'm so proud of this song, and having Noah Kahan on it is just, it's such a gift, you know
i started this song on my back porch and I was thinking about how lucky I am to have men in my life that are just emotionally vulnerable
and I've been able to, like, cry with, and have these beautiful conversations with, and
I wanted to write a song to honor them, and really kind of talk about toxic masculinity, and
having Noah write his side of, of that story into the song, it just brought it to a different level, and
it's so, it's believable now, because it's one thing to hear a woman talk about it
but it's another thing to have the male perspective, uhm, you know, write his sidе into it
and I'm just, I'm so proud of it, I really am, I love this song
